title: Trump’s Un-American Capitalism
gist: Joseph Stiglitz argues the Trump administration practices an openly corrupt crony capitalism that is the antithesis of the institutional foundation of the US economy, where competitive winners are those best at flattering the president rather than those who make the best products.

insights.claim: The Trump administration's openly corrupt crony capitalism is the antithesis of the institutional foundation on which the US economy was built.
insights.speaker: Joseph E. Stiglitz
insights.so_what: Erosion of impartial institutions raises the long-run discount on US growth and rule-of-law premia for investors.
insights.quote: The current US administration's openly corrupt style of crony capitalism is the antithesis of the institutional foundation on which the US economy was built.

insights.claim: In the new oligarchic competition, the winners are those best at flattering the president rather than those who make the best products, distorting capital allocation toward political favor.
insights.speaker: Joseph E. Stiglitz
insights.so_what: Political-access advantage over product merit is a signal to scrutinize firms whose moats depend on government favor.
insights.quote: The winners in the new oligarchic competition are not those who make the best products, but rather those who are best at flattering the mad king.
insights.locator: Standfirst, 'The winners in the new oligarchic competition'

insights.claim: Stiglitz contends Trump's Republican Party no longer genuinely supports capitalism or free enterprise despite its rhetoric, contradicting its stated anti-socialist stance.
insights.speaker: Joseph E. Stiglitz
insights.so_what: If the historically pro-market party shifts toward interventionism/favoritism, expect more discretionary, less predictable economic policy.
insights.quote: Far from continuing to champion capitalism and free enterprise, Trump's GOP no longer supports either.
